title: Why We Still Need a CIO in the AI-First Era
author: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/jean-francois-bobier

Tech + Us: Harness the power of technology and AI

You are subscribed to BCG's Tech + Us newsletter.

Manage Subscriptions

Build capacity is becoming abundant. Context, control, value steering, and digital-labor orchestration are becoming scarce. That is why the CIO role is becoming more important—and more different.AI is doing more than simply giving developers better tools. It is changing the constraints around which companies have organized technology for decades. Autonomous agents are moving from assisting individual tasks and could become more critical to planning, building, testing, deploying, and operating software. Engineering capacity is in short supply, just as organizations are also short on business intent, the quality of enterprise context, the coherence of the technology estate, and the ability to control digital labor at speed.That shift can make the traditional CIO role—which first took root in the early 1980s—look outdated. Business teams can create agents and applications directly. Software delivery can be accelerated by dark software factories where human teams define intent and review outcomes rather than write every line of code. AI consumption turns part of IT economics from fixed investment into usage-based OpEx. Geopolitical, regulatory, and cyber pressures require more localized and resilient architectures. In this environment, the CIO can no longer rely on a monopoly over technology delivery.But those same forces make the CIO more necessary. When everyone can build, someone must decide what should be built. When agents can act across workflows, someone must manage the digital labor lifecycle. When enterprise knowledge becomes machine-readable context, someone must ensure that data, rules, decisions, and system dependencies are accurate, governed, and reusable. When AI spend scales with every prompt, token, agent, and workflow, someone must link cost to value. And when controls must operate at machine speed, someone must embed them into platforms, not only review them after the fact.The mandate is expanding from service provider to orchestrator of enterprise intelligence and business outcomes. In some organizations, this mandate will be shared across the CIO, CIDO, CDO, and CTO. The title matters less than the mandate: make AI a business agenda, build the foundations that let it scale safely, and ensure that abundant build capacity turns into measurable value rather than complexity, risk, and cost.This is a critical moment for CIOs. The enterprise can only become AI-first if technology leaders stop managing scarcity and start orchestrating intent, context, digital labor, value, and control.

Tech + Us: Harness the power of technology and AI

Start Orchestrating the AI-First Enterprise
The shift to AI-first happens when the CIO stops optimizing the old technology operating model and starts building the new one.

Orchestrate Enterprise Intelligence

Steer Value Pathways

Codify Enterprise Knowledge

Manage Digital Labor

Embed Control at Machine Speed

Build an AI-Ready and Resilient Tech Estate

Orchestrate Enterprise Intelligence

Orchestrate Enterprise Intelligence

Stop delivering and running business applications end-to-end as a service provider that translates specifications into code and operations.Start building the enterprise AI engine: a shared agentic platform and dark software factory that can translate intent into software, workflows, and operational support with human oversight.The CIO should define the AI stack, platform architecture, orchestration layer, model routing, memory, evaluation, observability, and cost controls. Since the operating environment is moving so quickly, the goal is not to pick one tool or one model provider. Companies need a main stack that can scale with hyperscalers and enterprise platforms, plus optionality through open-source, on-premises, and specialist capabilities where sovereignty, resilience, or performance requires them. The platform must make AI delivery faster while making security, compliance, and economics visible by design.

Steer Value Pathways

Steer Value Pathways

Stop prioritizing based primarily on budget utilization, backlog pressure, or value-per-effort logic from an era of scarce build capacity.Start steering by value-per-complexity and value-per-token: the value delivered by a workflow, agent, or application relative to the coherence load, risk surface, cognitive burden, and run cost it adds.When agents make more work buildable, the question is no longer Can we build the application? The question is, Should it exist, and how will it improve the P&L? The CIO must work with the CEO, CFO, and business leaders to define AI value pathways around end-to-end outcomes. This includes tracking agent cost, human capacity freed up, business KPIs improved, and complexity added. Initial AI investments may need central funding, but the operating model must become value-funded as benefits are captured and reinvested. A strong CIO becomes the driver of a synthetic P&L for human-agent operations: ensuring agent growth is offset by lower human OpEx, higher revenue, better risk control, or faster renewal.

Codify Enterprise Knowledge

Codify Enterprise Knowledge

Stop letting the business express demand only through tickets, local requirements, and fragmented data requests.Start moving to an intent-driven model backed by codified enterprise knowledge: a shared, governed source of context, rules, semantics, quality criteria, process models, system dependencies, and decision history that agents and humans can use.AI-first companies need more than data lakes. They need context that agents can reason with. The CIO, often with the CDO or CIDO, should build a data and digital platform that decouples systems of record from the data layer and the digital execution layer. A Digital Data Platform approach turns raw enterprise data into reusable, permissioned context through data products, semantic layers, lineage, access controls, and strong handling of unstructured information. The AI layer then tracks model gardens, agentic systems, MCP servers where relevant, observability, and feedback loops. This positions the CIO as the company’s knowledge orchestrator: the leader who makes enterprise intelligence usable at scale.

Manage Digital Labor

Manage Digital Labor

Stop treating business-built agents as experiments, shadow IT, or tools that can be left to each function.Start managing agents as digital labor across a full lifecycle: design, approval, onboarding, permissioning, performance management, monitoring, retraining, redeployment, and retirement.The organization may soon run many more agents than employees. That creates a new accountability gap. Who knows which agents exist, what they are allowed to do, which data they use, what decisions they made, and when they should be shut down? CIOs must help design the hybrid human-agent operating model. Business intent owners define what should happen and validate outcomes. Technical orchestrators validate architecture, integration, and quality. Context engineers keep enterprise knowledge accurate and machine-readable. Harness engineers build and tune the runtimes, guardrails, pipelines, monitoring, and integration layers. Delivery lines become smaller human teams that steer elastic agent pools, rather than large teams whose capacity scales mainly with headcount.

Embed Control at Machine Speed

Embed Control at Machine Speed

Stop relying on manual governance gates, static policies, and after-the-fact reviews that cannot keep pace with agent-driven delivery.Start embedding programmable guardrails, proactive controls, and reactive audits into the AI platform, software factory, and production environment.A CIO will never want to slow innovation; their role moving forward is to make safe scaling possible. AI governance must move from committee cadence to system cadence. Architecture standards, security policies, data and privacy rules, responsible AI requirements, and regulatory obligations should be codified so agents can check work before code is written, during construction, and in operation. The CIO should maintain a repository of agents and models, define evaluation standards, and work with legal, compliance, risk, and procurement to manage regulation, third-party exposure, and vendor dependencies. FinOps must also become AI FinOps: managing token consumption, inference cost, agent activity, and unit economics against business value.

Build an AI-Ready and Resilient Tech Estate

Build an AI-Ready and Resilient Tech Estate

Stop assuming that optimization means maximum globalization, offshoring, outsourcing, and elimination of redundancy.Start continuously modernizing the technology estate so AI systems can connect, scale, localize, and recover safely.Agentic AI can accumulate legacy in real time. Every new workflow, API, agent, data dependency, and policy exception can become tomorrow’s complexity before the enterprise has absorbed it. The CIO must therefore treat modernization as a continuous function, not a periodic program. This means that leaders need to focus on refactoring brittle systems, simplifying integration, improving documentation, and reducing dependency on suppliers that cannot demonstrate resilience. CIOs also need to balance global scale with regional requirements for areas like sovereignty and regulation. In the AI-first enterprise, resilience is the ability to keep human-agent operations safe, compliant, explainable, and economically sustainable under stress.

People and Performance Drive Change
CIOs also need to lead the human side. The transformation to AI-first calls for organizational rewiring that no one officer or department naturally owns. Yet there’s a clear need for leadership, because much of the workforce will spend less time implementing and more time managing, contextualizing, validating, and governing. This allows the CIO to help define new roles, new skills, and new measures. Exhibits 1 and 2 lay out some of these changes.

1 / 2

CIOs should work with HR and business leaders to train teams in intent writing, agent orchestration, context curation, technical review, evaluation, and AI risk management. The organization should measure progress with metrics that reflect the new operating model, such as:value-per-complexity for the AI portfoliocost per outcome and cost per token for agentic operationsintent-to-production cycle timeagent output acceptance ratecontext freshness and reuseguardrail exceptions and policy violationsshare of workflows redesigned end-to-end, rather than task-automatedlegacy renewal rate and resilience performanceActions for the CIO Mandate
To move forward, CIOs and their colleagues should focus on several actions that translate the keynote angle into an executive mandate:Align the C-suite around AI as a business agenda. Build fluency and shared conviction on where agentic AI matters, what it takes to win, and which risks must be managed at ExCom level.Build AI value pathways with the business. Shift from isolated use cases to redesigned end-to-end workflows that connect agent choices to measurable business outcomes.Create scalable AI foundations for the enterprise. Define the AI platform, dark software factory, data and digital platform, integration architecture, observability, evaluation, and cost controls required for safe scale.Lead data, context, and knowledge governance. Turn enterprise data, process knowledge, system dependencies, and decision history into reusable, permissioned context for agents and teams.Manage the digital labor ecosystem. Maintain visibility over agents, models, permissions, owners, performance, and lifecycle decisions, while clarifying human accountability.Embed governance, cybersecurity, and compliance. Codify architecture, security, data, responsible AI, and regulatory requirements into guardrails, audits, and production controls.Rewire IT talent and roles. Build the capabilities needed for business intent owners, technical orchestrators, context engineers, harness engineers, and agent-assisted delivery teams.Orchestrate the AI vendor ecosystem jointly with procurement. Manage hyperscalers, model providers, open-source options, integration partners, and specialist vendors with a focus on optionality, resilience, and economics.Manage technology cost for value. Track AI consumption, inference cost, agent activity, complexity, and benefits so AI scales impact faster than OpEx.Continuously renew the technology estate. Treat legacy modernization, resilience, and regionalization as ongoing requirements of an AI-first enterprise, not as episodic remediation programs.The CIO mandate in many organizations still reflects the priorities of the digital era. In the AI-first era, the bigger risk is different: technology becomes too easy to create, too fragmented to govern, too expensive to run, and too complex to renew.CIOs who hold on to the old monopoly over scarce IT delivery will lose relevance. CIOs who become orchestrators of enterprise intelligence will earn a broader mandate. They will shape the C-suite agenda, define value pathways, manage digital labor, codify context, embed controls, and keep the technology estate resilient.The enterprise still needs a CIO because AI-first needs a clear owner of the system that turns intent into outcomes safely and economically. The CEO and ExCom should reset the mandate accordingly: roles, decision rights, governance, funding, and metrics. The companies that succeed will not be those with the most agents. They will be those with the best-orchestrated system of people, agents, context, controls, and value.
